name: Her Labour, Her Law
description:
This session is in English and Kannada. 
Domestic work keeps the country running. But the women who make it possible, often after tending to their own homes first, have no contracts, no fixed hours, and no legal protection. 
The proposed Domestic Workers Bill in Karnataka aims to regulate the industry, bringing in protections enjoyed by employees in the formal sector. This panel examines what the Bill actually means: for the women it seeks to protect, for the households that depend on them, and for a labour movement that has long treated domestic work as an afterthought. 
A Q&A with the audience will follow. 
This event is a part of our programming to celebrate International Women’s Day. Music, theatre, and conversation: by women, for everyone.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Dance With Space
description:
Five decades inside a form leaves its knowledge in the body. This evening, Malavika Sarukkai brings it to you. 
One of India’s foremost Bharatanatyam artistes, Sarukkai presents a selection of her choreographies alongside the creative process that shaped them, the layering of intention and intuition, the place where classical grammar meets a contemporary mind. Accompanied by four musicians, she moves between demonstration and reflection, making visible what usually remains privy to the artist alone. 
The evening is open to all: rasikas, dancers, scholars, and enthusiasts alike. 
Photo credits: Mayur Channagere 
Managed by Shreya Nagarajan Singh Arts Development Consultancy. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: What She Said
description:
Language: English 
The Ramayana remembers everyone. Just not equally. 
In six monologues, Gowri Ramnarayan fashions the women the epic found convenient to sideline into individuals in their own right, each voice as complex, as intelligent, and as worthy of attention as Sita’s own. 
Some kept faith across impossible distances: Urmila, who waited while her husband followed his brother into exile; Shanta, Rama’s sister, given away twice and nearly forgotten. 
Some loved with a ferocity that changed everything: Manthara, whose devotion to Kaikeyi set the entire epic in motion; Surpanakha, who desired without apology and made the epic answer for what it did to her. 
And some endured what would have broken lesser women: Tara, who survived her husband’s murder and stayed to protect her son; Mandodari, who watched Ravana take his entire world down with him and bore witness to the end with her eyes open. 
Six women. Finally, the floor is theirs. 
This event is a part of our programming to celebrate International Women’s Day. Music, theatre, and conversation: by women, for everyone. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Entangled Worlds
description:
Science has never been apolitical. It has just been silent about politics and social identities. 
This talk traces the entanglements between nature and society across thirty years of field research in India and Latin America, moving between blackbuck conservation in Maharashtra, iguana habitats in Costa Rica, fisheries in the Brazilian Amazon, Afro-Colombian movements, and pastoralist struggles in Kutch. 
What connects them is not geography but a single multiple entanglements of culture, nature, and political economy. 
A Q&A will follow the lecture.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Leading with Calm
description:
In an engaging conversation, Sanjiv Sarin discusses his recently launched book ‘The Other Side of the Mountain’ with accomplished writer and corporate leader Harish Bhat, reflecting on his journey into spiritual learning and its impact on his corporate life. 
The book has received extremely positive reviews from business leaders, media and readers. 
Sanjiv worked in the corporate world for over forty years in various leadership roles across the globe, retiring as CEO and MD of Tata Coffee. Earlier he had worked across the globe with Cadbury. In this time, he encountered many challenges and learned many life lessons. But his biggest learning came when he embarked on a spiritual path that took him deep into The Art of Living as a practitioner and then as a Teacher. 
A life-changing moment occurred with the terrorist attack at the Taj Mahal Hotel on 26/11, when he was staying there on a business trip. After his rescue, Sanjiv understood even more clearly the value of mindful living, meditation, and a calm mind. He moved from being a practitioner and Teacher to a life of introspection and self-application of all the learnings. He experienced first-hand the value of being a spiritual practitioner while remaining firmly in the world of business. 
The Other Side of the Mountain chronicles Sanjiv’s learnings in spirituality and how he has used them successfully in leadership situations. Full of wisdom, humour and self-realisation, this book is for anyone trying to make sense of the modern competitive world and their role in it. As one of the readers said “….A thought provoking integration of mind and heart, which should be the new art of Management….” 
The book will be launched and introduced by Bhaskar Bhat. Sanjiv will be in conversation with Harish Bhat. A Q&A with the audience will follow.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Reframing Cities for Resilience
description:
We live in times of profuse change, where hyperconnectivity and fast-shifting influences are radically changing how we live, consume, work, and play. By challenging the traditional realms in which urban design and planning operate, this new book by Arun Jain asserts that not only must we do better, but that we can. 
Arun argues that the first step toward adaptive and resilient futures is making a fuller spectrum of the complexity of cities comprehensible to all. Drawing upon diverse disciplines like cultural anthropology, evolutionary biology, economic theory, behavioral sciences, systems thinking, and technology, he provides fresh foundations for addressing decades of physical and social underinvestment and neglect across a range of urban challenges. 
This book is essential for urban designers, planners, landscape architects, and postgraduate students in these fields. 
Join us for an engaging and thought-provoking introduction to the book by its author, Arun Jain, followed by a discussion with Srikanth Viswanathan and an audience Q&A. 
Presented by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Living Authentically
description:
Courage is not the beginning of self-knowledge. It is the constant condition of it. 
Courage to Be You follows two people asking that question from very different lives. Amit and Najib are a gay couple navigating not only their relationship but the religious and social distance between them. Rajeshwari, Amit’s childhood friend, is quietly working to reclaim an identity fractured by early trauma. Their stories run parallel, but their questions are the same: what does it take to choose yourself, and what do you risk when you do? 
Rashna Imhasly-Gandhy draws on Jungian psychology and chakra healing, offering the reader a framework for the same inner work her characters undertake. Their guide, in both cases, is a therapist. Their destination is their own. 
Yasmeen Premji will introduce the session. Rashna’s presentation will be followed by a conversation between her and Malini White.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: The Long Game: Opportunity, Excellence and Institutional Power
description:
Opportunity is abundant. Outcomes, however, are strategic.  
The four women on this panel have navigated all of it. Venture capital and enterprise strategy. Global talent transformation. The slow, necessary work of making the technology industry legible to the women coming up behind them. Startups built from scratch and careers that kept becoming something new.  
They have done this inside institutions that weren’t built for them, in markets that moved before they were ready, in moments that required a decision before the picture was clear. Together they examine how signals and opportunities are recognised, and sometimes missed. How excellence is sustained over decades, not just built in a moment. How to navigate boards, capital, and institutions. How and when to seek help from the mentors, sponsors, and allies who make the difference.  
The answers come from lived experience. The lessons, hard-won as they are, turn out to be universal.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Has Trump 2.0 Commandeered India’s Foreign Policy?
description:
When the world shifts, what anchors a nation’s choices? 
The Bengaluru launch of India’s Tryst With the World: A Foreign Policy Manifesto arrives at a moment of visible churn in India’s external relations. With shifts in United States policy under a second Trump presidency, long-assumed alignments have grown uncertain; prompting rapid recalibrations toward China, renewed invocations of friendship with Russia, and fresh questions about strategic autonomy. The book frames this moment not as disruption alone, but as an opportunity to rethink how India engages the world. 
Co-edited by Salman Khurshid and Salil Shetty, the volume sets the stage for a wide-ranging conversation on diplomacy, values, and global responsibility. With Nirupama Rao and Suhasini Haider in conversation and Latha Reddy moderating, the evening reflects on India’s foreign policy record and its evolving role in a post-Trump international order, inviting careful listening, debate, and reflection as the contours of the future come into view. 
A Q&A with the audience will follow.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Ultimate Secrets of Wild Poop
description:
Poop is a bad word. It is also, depending on who you ask, a home, a weapon, a meal, and a way to save the planet. 
In the animal kingdom, dung is serious business. Herbivores use scat the way we use social media. Tigers use it to mark territory. Dung beetles build homes in it and eat it for breakfast. Hoopoes weaponise it. Blue whales poop in quantities that actually help keep our oceans alive. And scientists collect all of it, enthusiastically, in the name of conservation. 
Join author Shweta Taneja for a noisy, messy, gloriously disgusting science session on wild animals and what they leave behind. 
Warning: you will be grossed out, you will giggle, and you will learn things you cannot unlearn. 

Around the World in 50 Sundays is a journey that begins, not with a ticket, but with opening a book. In a city like Bangalore where families are always in motion, the BIC Library hopes to offer stillness and a quiet, welcoming space for both parents and children where reading becomes a shared way of being and not a task. 
Every Sunday, we open our doors to invite you into a library that listens; building a rhythm of return, a habit of joy and a new community bound not just by books, but by the weekly ritual of reading together. 

Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Beyond Accompaniment
description:
The harmonium has spent centuries being useful. 
It has held the shruti, supported the vocalist, filled the space between phrases with something steady and unobtrusive. It has been, in the best possible sense, reliable. What it has rarely been given is the stage to itself. 
This recital does exactly that. The programme traverses khayal, thumri, tappa, dhun, theatre music, and devotional compositions, moving between gayaki ang and tantrakari ang, the vocal and instrumental traditions. One segment demonstrates how a shift in genre changes the technique, the phrasing, the entire interpretive logic of the instrument. The kind of detail that makes you realise you have never quite listened to it before. 
The harmonium, it turns out, has been waiting. And this is its debut. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: The Cities We Carry
description:
Two questions plagued her for five years, living between Delhi, Venice, and Istanbul: how do we lose what we lose, and why do we love whom we love? 
Drawing on her collection of essays Place: Intimate Encounters with Cities (2026), written across 25 years and 13 cities, Ananya Vajpeyi will explore what cities hold for us.  The memory and meaning held in street corners and corridors of light; the way personal loss and political trouble have of finding each other in the very same spaces. 
Architect Prem Chandavarkar brings his own lifelong reckoning with cities to the table. Between them, the conversation promises to move across continents, disciplines, and the question of what it means to love a place. 
The conversation will be followed by a Q&A with the audience.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Colors of Hindustani Slide Guitar
description:
The slide guitar was not born into Hindustani classical music. Joel ‘Veena’ Eisenkramer has spent a lifetime bringing it home. 
Eisenkramer has spent decades inside the Hindustani oral tradition, in conversation with musicians, collecting the stories and lore that live between the notes. This evening, he brings all of it to the stage: traditional ragas and original compositions, interwoven with stories told as they were meant to be, spoken and spontaneous. Anand HS accompanies on tabla. 
An evening that asks something of the listener, and returns considerably more. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: The Battle Royale
description:
87 min | 2025, India, Germany | Bengali, English and Hindi with English Subtitles 
On 8 December 2023, TMC MP Mahua Moitra was unfairly removed from Indian Parliament. It was alleged without evidence that she had accepted ‘cash for query’, or financial inducements to raise questions in Parliament against Gautam Adani, a billionaire businessman considered close to PM Narendra Modi and the ruling BJP. 
The Battle Royale tracks Mahua Moitra’s re-election campaign in the high stakes battleground constituency of Krishnanagar as she fights for her political survival and for redemption and retribution. Pitted against her in a close three – cornered contest are the CPI(M)/INC candidate, S.M. Sadi, and the ‘Rajmata’ (royal mother) of Krishnanagar, Amrita Roy, who is supported by the mighty BJP. 
 The Battle Royale is part of Election Diaries 2024 , a series of 9 documentaries produced by CeMIS (Centre for Modern Indian Studies) that reflect the anxieties, the hopes and the resilience of Indian democracy over the 2024 elections

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Celebrating Women Composers
description:
Sthree Thaal Tharang is a soulful celebration of women in music. 
The repertoire draws from the vachanas of Akkamahadevi, addressed to Chenna Mallikarjuna and marked by spiritual intensity and metaphysical longing; the verses of Andal, whose poetic imagination and theological vision shaped Tamil bhakti literature; and the bhajans of Mirabai, whose devotion was fearless, deeply personal, and entirely her own. 
The programme moves into the twentieth century through Ambujam Krishna, whose lyrical clarity and devotional depth brought a different kind of rigour to the form, and through compositions popularised by pioneering women performers whose interpretations shaped modern concert practice. The morning culminates in laya-oriented compositions by Sukanya Ramgopal, where ghata tharang meets percussive texture and melodic interplay. 
Sthree Thaal Tharang situates women not at the margins but at the centre of music’s evolving history: composers, interpreters, and custodians of a living tradition. 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India
